plainMeaning: Holding the Arhat as my god, true monks as my teachers, and the Jina-taught path as my religion, I here check my right faith and my first two householder vows — non-violence and truthfulness — naming the five small slips that can stain each and begging pardon for whatever I slipped into today.
recitation: (icchaṁ) arihanta deva, susādhu guru, jina-praṇīta dharma, bhāvataḥ samakita pratipāluṁ, dravyato laukika, lokottara, devagata, gurugata, parvagata mithyātva viṣe jayaṇā karuṁ. e śrī samakita-taṇā pāṁca atiyāra śodhuṁ. śaṅkā, kaṅkhā, vitigicchā, para-pākhaṇḍī praśaṁsā, para-pākhaṇḍī saṁthuo. e pāṁca atiyāra-māṁhe divasa (rātri hoya to "rātri sambandhī" kahevuṁ) sambandhī jiko koi atiyāra huo hoya, te savi huṁ mane, vacane, kāyāe karī micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ. 1. bāra vrata māṁhe paheluṁ prāṇātipāta viramaṇa-vrata. sthūla be indriyādika trasa jīva niraparādha upetakaraṇa saṅkalpī karī haṇavā niyama, ārambhe jayaṇā. e pahelā prāṇātipāta viramaṇa-vrata-taṇā pāṁca atiyāra śodhuṁ. bandhe, vahe, chavicchee, aibhāre, bhatta-pāṇa-vucchee. e pāṁca atiyāra-māṁhe divasa sambandhī. ityādi pāṭha dareka atiyāre kahevuṁ. 2. bījuṁ sthūla mṛṣāvāda viramaṇa-vrata. pañcavidha kannālīe, govālīe, bhamālīe, nāsāvahāre, kūḍasakkhijje. e pāṁca moṭakāṁ kūḍāṁ āpaṇane kāje, svajanane kāje, dharmane kāje mūkī, parakāje kūḍuṁ bolavā niyama, sūkṣma alīka-taṇī jayaṇā karuṁ. e bījā sthūla mṛṣāvāda viramaṇa-vrata-taṇā pāṁca atiyāra śodhuṁ. sahassābhakkhāṇo, rahassābhakkhāṇo, sadārā-manta-bhee, mosovaese, kūḍalehakaraṇe. e pāṁca atiyāra māṁhe.

wordByWord.gloss: "I assent / I am willing" — the assent-word answering the guru's command
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit icchaṁ from Sanskrit icchā (wish, assent), root iṣ 'to desire'
wordByWord.notes: Bracketed in the book as the ādeśa-response carried over from the preceding vidhi.

wordByWord.gloss: the Arhat (the Conqueror-of-foes, the worshipful Jina)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit arihanta from Sanskrit arhat 'worthy'; popularly etymologised ari-hanta 'slayer of inner enemies'
wordByWord.notes: Subject of the credal affirmation: the Arhat alone is 'deva'.
Predicate: defines true devatā against the false 'devagata mithyātva' below.

wordByWord.etymology: su- 'good' + sādhu 'ascetic, accomplished one' (root sādh 'to accomplish')

wordByWord.notes: Qualifies dharma.
The triad deva–guru–dharma is the content of samyaktva.

wordByWord.gloss: from the standpoint of inner disposition (bhāva)
wordByWord.notes: Paired with dravyato below — the dravya/bhāva (outer/inner) analysis.

wordByWord.etymology: vernacular form of Sanskrit samyaktva via Prakrit sammatta

wordByWord.notes: Inwardly (bhāvataḥ) the reciter resolves to keep right faith.

wordByWord.gloss: from the standpoint of outward substance/conduct (dravya)
wordByWord.notes: Outwardly the reciter must still exercise jayaṇā toward the four false-belief contacts.
First of the four spheres of contact with mithyātva.
These four qualify mithyātva: worldly, trans-worldly, god-, guru- and festival-related false belief.
The direct opposite of samakita; the thing toward which only jayaṇā, not free participation, is allowed.

wordByWord.gloss: with regard to / in the matter of
wordByWord.notes: Postposition governing mithyātva.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit jayaṇā from Sanskrit yatanā 'careful effort' (√yat)
wordByWord.notes: Defined in the preface; here it means careful avoidance of unavoidable contact with false belief.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati demonstrative e from Prakrit eya/eta
wordByWord.notes: Recurs as the template-opener 'e … atiyāra śodhuṁ'.
Reverential prefix to samakita.

wordByWord.etymology: book's spelling of atichāra; ati- 'beyond' + cāra 'conduct' (√car)
wordByWord.notes: Glossary term; book retains the ય spelling.
The refrain-verb of self-examination repeated at each vow.
1st atichāra of samyaktva (Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.18).

wordByWord.gloss: craving (kāṅkṣā — hankering after other creeds/their rewards)
wordByWord.notes: 2nd atichāra of samyaktva.

wordByWord.gloss: doubting disgust (vicikitsā — wavering / squeamishness about the fruit of dharma)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit vitigicchā from Sanskrit vicikitsā 'doubt, repugnance'
wordByWord.notes: 3rd atichāra of samyaktva.
Modifies the next two atichāras.
4th atichāra: para-pākhaṇḍī-praśaṁsā.
Repeated to introduce the fifth atichāra.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit saṁthuo from Sanskrit saṁstava 'praise, close acquaintance' (sam-√stu)
wordByWord.notes: 5th atichāra: para-pākhaṇḍī-saṁstava.

wordByWord.etymology: atiyāra + māṁhe (locative postposition 'within', from Prakrit majjhe/madhye)

wordByWord.notes: The Devasi (evening) form; the bracket below gives the Rāi substitution.
Opens the bracketed substitution cue.

wordByWord.etymology: vernacular hoya from Prakrit hoi (√bhū 'to be')

wordByWord.notes: Quoted substitute wording.
i.e. say 'rātri sambandhī' (night-related) instead of 'divasa'.

wordByWord.etymology: vernacular kahevuṁ from Prakrit kaha- (√kath 'to tell'); gerundive
wordByWord.notes: Closes the bracketed vidhi-cue.
Resumes the main sentence: 'whatever day-related transgression…'.

wordByWord.etymology: old Gujarati relative jiko 'whichever' (from Prakrit jo)

wordByWord.notes: huo hoya = 'should have happened' (potential).

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati huṁ from Prakrit ahaṁ (Sanskrit aham)

wordByWord.notes: First of the three-fold instrument (mind, speech, body).
Mind–speech–body = the threefold yoga by which fault is incurred.
i.e. committed through mind, speech and body.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit micchā from Sanskrit mithyā 'wrongly, vainly'
wordByWord.notes: Part of the sealing formula; glossary term.
Completes 'micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ', the confession-seal of the samyaktva unit.
Ordinal marking the first of the twelve vows.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati pahelo from Prakrit paḍhama (Sanskrit prathama)

wordByWord.notes: First aṇuvrata: abstaining from harm to life.
Marks the aṇuvrata as 'gross' as opposed to the monk's total (sūkṣma) abstention.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati be from Prakrit be/duve (Sanskrit dvi)

wordByWord.notes: i.e. beings from two-sensed up to five-sensed — the mobile (trasa) jīvas the householder vows not to kill.
Opposed to sthāvara (immobile, one-sensed); only trasa-jīvas are protected by the lay vow.
Defines the scope: the vow covers innocent mobile beings.
Reinforces saṅkalpī: only purposeful killing is renounced (variant readings exist; see notes).
The lay vow forbids saṅkalpa-jā hiṁsā (intentional killing), not unavoidable household harm.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit haṇ- from Sanskrit √han 'to strike, kill'; infinitive -vā

wordByWord.notes: The firm restriction: not to kill innocent mobile beings deliberately.
Unavoidable occupational harm; here only jayaṇā (care), not total abstention, applies.
The niyama/jayaṇā balance of the householder's vow.
1st atichāra of vow 1 (bandha) — Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.20.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit vaha from Sanskrit vadha 'slaying, beating' (√vadh)
wordByWord.notes: 2nd atichāra (vadha).
3rd atichāra (chaviccheda).
4th atichāra (atibhāra).
5th atichāra (annapāna-nirodha).
Refrain abbreviated; the day/night confession is understood.
Opens a vidhi-instruction, not verse content.
Vidhi-cue: repeat the full day/night confession at every vow.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati bījuṁ from Prakrit bīa (Sanskrit dvitīya)

wordByWord.notes: Second aṇuvrata: truthfulness.
Introduces the five classical 'great lies' renounced under this vow.
1st of the five gross lies — false testimony in marriage/bipeds.
2nd gross lie — false testimony about quadrupeds.
3rd gross lie — false testimony about land/property.
4th gross lie — denying goods left in trust.
5th gross lie — perjury / bearing false witness.
Qualifies the 'great lies'.
First of three motives the reciter forswears as grounds for the great lies.
Even pious motive does not license a great lie.
i.e. renouncing the great lies even on these three pretexts.

wordByWord.gloss: for the sake of others / for any other purpose

wordByWord.notes: Firm rule against gross lying.
Opposed to the gross (sthūla) lie; toward subtle untruth only jayaṇā applies.
1st atichāra of vow 2 — Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.21.
2nd atichāra — disclosing or fabricating on confidential matters.
3rd atichāra — divulging a spouse's/partner's confidences.
4th atichāra.
5th atichāra — forgery/false records.
Verse closes mid-template; the 'divasa sambandhī … micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ' refrain is supplied as before.

literalTranslation: (I assent.) The Arhat is (my) god, the true sādhu (my) guru, the Jina-taught path (my) dharma — inwardly (bhāvataḥ) I will uphold right faith; outwardly (dravyato) I exercise prudent caution regarding worldly, trans-worldly, god-related, guru-related and festival-related false belief. I scrutinise the five transgressions of this holy right faith: doubt, craving, sceptical disgust, praise of other-sectarians, intimate lauding of other-sectarians. Among these five, whatever day-related (if night, say 'night-related') transgression of mine has occurred — all of it, done by mind, speech and body — may my misdeed be undone. 1. Among the twelve vows, the first is the vow of abstaining from killing. I take the rule not to deliberately, with intent, kill innocent mobile beings (two-sensed and upward); in (unavoidable) household activity I keep (only) prudent caution. I scrutinise the five transgressions of this first abstaining-from-killing vow: binding, beating, mutilating, overloading, withholding food and drink. Among these five, day-related… (recite the same passage at each transgression). 2. The second is the gross vow of abstaining from false speech. The five-fold (great lies are): false witness about a maiden, about cattle, about land, misappropriation of a deposit, and perjury. Setting aside (the temptation to tell) these five great lies for our own sake, for kin's sake, or for religion's sake, I take the rule not to speak falsehood for any (other) purpose; toward subtle untruth I keep prudent caution. I scrutinise the five transgressions of this second gross vow of abstaining from false speech: rash accusation, betrayal of secrets, betraying a spouse's counsel, false instruction, forging a false document. Among these five…
idiomaticTranslation: I take the Arhat as my only God, true monks as my only teachers, and the path the Jina taught as my only religion — and inwardly I vow to keep that faith whole. Outwardly I will guard myself with care wherever false belief meets me: in worldly custom, in counterfeit 'higher' religion, in false gods, false gurus, false holy-days. Let me now search my faith for its five stains — doubt, hankering after other creeds, squeamish wavering, praising the heterodox, courting their company — and for any such slip made today by thought, word or deed, may my wrongdoing be undone. First vow: not to kill. I bind myself never to slay, of set purpose, any innocent moving creature; in the unavoidable work of a household I will at least move with care. Its five stains I search out: tethering, beating, maiming, overloading, starving a creature of food and water. Second vow: not to lie. I will not utter, for my own gain, my family's, or even religion's, any of the five great falsehoods — false oaths over a bride, over cattle, over land, denying a trust, bearing false witness — and toward smaller untruths I keep watchful care. Its five stains I search out: rash slander, betraying confidences, exposing a spouse's secrets, false counsel, and forgery.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This opening verse does something the rest of the sūtra never repeats: before touching conduct (cāritra) it audits faith (darśana). The reciter first makes the threefold creed explicit — Arhat as deva, susādhu as guru, jina-praṇīta as dharma — the classic Jain definition of samyaktva as confidence in true god, true teacher, true teaching. The verse then splits the self along the dravya/bhāva axis the preface flagged: 'bhāvataḥ samakita pratipāluṁ' (inwardly I keep faith whole) but 'dravyato … jayaṇā karuṁ' (outwardly I merely exercise care). This is realistic, not lax: a householder cannot avoid all contact with laukika custom, 'higher'-sounding rival creeds, or popular god-, guru- and festival-cults, so the vow asks for inner purity plus outward vigilance. Five faith-stains follow — śaṅkā, kāṅkṣā, vicikitsā, para-pākhaṇḍī-praśaṁsā, para-pākhaṇḍī-saṁstava — exactly the samyaktva-aticāras of Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.18, here in the book's Prākrit dress. Only then does the count of vows begin. Vows 1 and 2 immediately display the verse's governing logic: each pairs a niyama (firm rule) with a jayaṇā (leeway). For non-violence the rule is narrow and precise — no saṅkalpī (premeditated) killing of niraparādha trasa (innocent mobile) beings — while ārambha-jā (occupational) harm is met only with care; its five aticāras (bandha, vadha, chaviccheda, atibhāra, anna-pāna-nirodha) are crimes of cruelty to captive creatures, not of outright slaughter, which is the point: even one who does not kill can still abuse. Truthfulness mirrors this: the gross lie (kanyā-, go-, bhūmi-alīka, deposit-theft, perjury) is renounced absolutely — even 'for religion's sake' — while subtle untruth gets jayaṇā, and its five aticāras (rash accusation, betrayed secrets, a spouse's exposed counsel, false counsel, forgery) are sins of the tongue that fall short of formal perjury yet corrode trust. The verse thus teaches the whole book's method in miniature: name the ideal, fix the rule, admit the leeway, then hunt the small betrayals.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse encodes the Jain axiom that right faith (samyag-darśana) precedes and grounds right conduct (samyak-cāritra): faith is examined first because vows kept without it bear no liberative fruit (cf. Tattvārtha-sūtra 1.1, the ratna-traya). Two structural doctrines surface. First, the dravya/bhāva (substance/disposition) analysis: the same act is judged by outer form and inner intent, and it is bhāva-hiṁsā/bhāva-mṛṣā — the inner lapse — that truly binds karma. Second, the aṇuvrata principle that a layperson's vow is partial (deśa-virati): the rule bites only on saṅkalpī (intentional) and sthūla (gross) violation, leaving ārambhī and sūkṣma to jayaṇā. The aticāra concept itself — a stain that weakens but does not break a vow — presupposes that a vow is a living gradient, not a binary, so that confession (ālocanā) can mend it. The five-plus-five lists are not random: they catalogue the indirect ways a vow leaks even when its headline prohibition is kept — one may not kill yet still tether and starve; one may not perjure yet still slander in haste.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited daily, this verse asks the practitioner to begin self-examination at the root — 'Did I this day let doubt, envy of other paths, or fashionable scorn quietly erode my conviction?' — before auditing actions. Its enduring lesson is that the vows fail not by dramatic breach but by small cruelties and small untruths: overloading a worker, withholding due food, a rash accusation, a betrayed confidence, a forged figure. The dravya/bhāva and niyama/jayaṇā framing also models honest ethics for a layperson who cannot withdraw from commerce or family — hold the inner standard absolute, meet unavoidable compromise with conscientious care, and confess the gap nightly rather than pretend it away.

notes: The verse spans the samyaktva preamble plus vows 1–2; the book numbers it '2' as a page-marker, not a vow-number (the operative count is the Gujarati ordinals ૧, ૨). 'upetakaraṇa' (ઉપેતકરણ) is read here as reinforcing saṅkalpī ('with intent'); the form is unusual and other recensions read 'uddiṣṭa' or omit it — flagged as a variant. The Prākrit aticāra-spellings (sahassābhakkhāṇo, sadārā-manta-bhee, kūḍasakkhijje, bhatta-pāṇa-vucchee) follow the book verbatim and differ slightly from Tattvārtha's Sanskrit (e.g. sākāra-mantra-bheda vs svadāra-mantra-bheda; the book's 'sadārā' supports the 'one's own wife' reading). Repeated template tokens (e, pāṁca, atiyāra, śodhuṁ, etc.) are glossed at each occurrence per the 100%-coverage rule. micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ is treated as three tokens (micchā / mi / dukkaḍaṁ) following the book's word-spacing. anchalgachchh.org is the tradition portal standing in for the scanned golden book; the verse text itself is the single golden source.
